content.text: How Containers Isolate Code

A Linux container is not a virtual machine — it shares the host kernel but uses kernel features to make the container process believe it is the only thing running on a separate system. Three mechanisms do the work: namespaces (what the process can see), cgroups (how much resource it can consume), and seccomp/capabilities (which syscalls and privileges it can use).
For code execution sandboxes, all three layers must be active. A container with generous cgroups and no seccomp filter can still exhaust CPU or call mount() to escape the filesystem namespace.

attrs.alt: Container isolation layers: cgroups (resource limits), namespaces (visibility), seccomp (syscall filter), capabilities (privilege drop)

content.text: Building a Minimal Execution Image

The sandbox image should have exactly the Python runtime and any required libraries — nothing else. Shells, package managers, and network tools increase the attack surface if code manages to escape AST-level filters:

# Dockerfile.sandbox
FROM python:3.12-slim AS base

# Install only what the agent needs
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir numpy pandas matplotlib scipy

# Remove attack-surface tools
RUN rm -f /bin/sh /bin/bash /usr/bin/curl /usr/bin/wget \
          /usr/bin/apt* /usr/bin/dpkg /usr/sbin/adduser

# Create a non-root user
RUN useradd --no-create-home --shell /bin/false sandbox

# Read-only filesystem by default; /tmp provided as tmpfs at runtime
USER sandbox
WORKDIR /tmp

ENTRYPOINT ["python3", "-c"]

# Build
docker build -f Dockerfile.sandbox -t code-sandbox:latest .

# Test isolation
docker run --rm --network none --memory 128m --read-only \
  --tmpfs /tmp:size=16m,noexec \
  --cap-drop ALL --security-opt no-new-privileges \
  code-sandbox:latest \
  "import numpy as np; print(np.arange(5))"
The Complete docker run Flag Set
Each flag addresses a specific attack vector. Understanding what each one does prevents accidentally omitting a critical one:
Flag
What it prevents
--network none
Outbound connections to internal services, exfiltration, C2 callbacks
--memory 128m --memory-swap 128m
Memory exhaustion OOM-killing other containers on the host
--cpu-quota 50000
CPU starvation of the host; 50000/100000 = 50% of one CPU
--pids-limit 64
Fork bombs; prevents spawning thousands of child processes
--read-only
Writes to container rootfs; forces code to use /tmp (tmpfs)
--tmpfs /tmp:size=16m,noexec
Disk exhaustion; noexec prevents compiling and running binaries from /tmp
--cap-drop ALL
CAP_SYS_ADMIN, CAP_NET_ADMIN, CAP_SYS_PTRACE and 30+ other capabilities
--security-opt no-new-privileges
SUID binaries escalating privileges after exec
--security-opt seccomp=profile.json
Dangerous syscalls: mount, kexec_load, add_key, ptrace
--user sandbox
Running as root inside the container (still root on kernel if namespace escape)
Running Containers from Python

import subprocess
import json
import uuid

SANDBOX_IMAGE = "code-sandbox:latest"

def run_in_container(
    code: str,
    timeout: int = 15,
    memory: str = "128m",
    network: str = "none",
) -> dict:
    """Execute Python code in a disposable Docker container."""
    container_name = f"sandbox-{uuid.uuid4().hex[:8]}"

    cmd = [
        "docker", "run",
        "--rm",
        "--name", container_name,
        "--network", network,
        "--memory", memory,
        "--memory-swap", memory,
        "--cpu-quota", "50000",
        "--pids-limit", "64",
        "--read-only",
        "--tmpfs", "/tmp:size=16m,noexec",
        "--cap-drop", "ALL",
        "--security-opt", "no-new-privileges",
        SANDBOX_IMAGE,
        code,
    ]

    try:
        proc = subprocess.run(
            cmd,
            capture_output=True,
            text=True,
            timeout=timeout + 5,  # extra for container startup
        )
        return {
            "stdout": proc.stdout[:8192],
            "stderr": proc.stderr[:2048],
            "returncode": proc.returncode,
            "error": None if proc.returncode == 0 else proc.stderr[:1024],
        }
    except subprocess.TimeoutExpired:
        # Kill the container if it is still running
        subprocess.run(["docker", "kill", container_name], capture_output=True)
        return {"stdout": "", "stderr": "", "returncode": -1,
                "error": f"Timed out after {timeout}s"}
    except FileNotFoundError:
        return {"stdout": "", "stderr": "", "returncode": -1,
                "error": "Docker daemon not available"}
Pre-warming Containers
Cold-starting a container for every code execution adds 1–3 seconds of startup latency. For interactive agents this is significant. Two patterns reduce it:
Container Pool

import asyncio
import subprocess
import uuid

class ContainerPool:
    """Maintain a pool of warm, idle sandbox containers."""

    def __init__(self, image: str, pool_size: int = 4):
        self.image = image
        self.pool_size = pool_size
        self._available: asyncio.Queue = asyncio.Queue()

    async def start(self):
        for _ in range(self.pool_size):
            cid = await self._spawn_container()
            await self._available.put(cid)

    async def _spawn_container(self) -> str:
        name = f"pool-{uuid.uuid4().hex[:8]}"
        proc = await asyncio.create_subprocess_exec(
            "docker", "run", "-d", "--rm",
            "--name", name,
            "--network", "none",
            "--memory", "128m",
            "--memory-swap", "128m",
            "--cpu-quota", "50000",
            "--pids-limit", "64",
            "--read-only",
            "--tmpfs", "/tmp:size=16m,noexec",
            "--cap-drop", "ALL",
            "--security-opt", "no-new-privileges",
            self.image, "sleep", "300",      # idle for 5 minutes
            stdout=asyncio.subprocess.PIPE,
        )
        stdout, _ = await proc.communicate()
        return stdout.decode().strip()

    async def run(self, code: str, timeout: int = 15) -> dict:
        container_id = await asyncio.wait_for(
            self._available.get(), timeout=5.0
        )
        try:
            proc = await asyncio.create_subprocess_exec(
                "docker", "exec", container_id,
                "python3", "-c", code,
                stdout=asyncio.subprocess.PIPE,
                stderr=asyncio.subprocess.PIPE,
            )
            stdout, stderr = await asyncio.wait_for(
                proc.communicate(), timeout=timeout
            )
            return {
                "stdout": stdout.decode()[:8192],
                "stderr": stderr.decode()[:2048],
                "error": None if proc.returncode == 0 else stderr.decode()[:1024],
            }
        except asyncio.TimeoutExpired:
            await asyncio.create_subprocess_exec("docker", "kill", container_id)
            container_id = await self._spawn_container()   # replace killed container
            return {"stdout": "", "stderr": "", "error": "Timed out"}
        finally:
            await self._available.put(container_id)  # return to pool

    async def stop(self):
        while not self._available.empty():
            cid = self._available.get_nowait()
            subprocess.run(["docker", "kill", cid], capture_output=True)
Seccomp Profiles
Docker's default seccomp profile blocks ~44 syscalls. For code execution sandboxes you can use a stricter custom profile. The key syscalls to additionally block are:

{
  "defaultAction": "SCMP_ACT_ERRNO",
  "architectures": ["SCMP_ARCH_X86_64", "SCMP_ARCH_X86", "SCMP_ARCH_X32"],
  "syscalls": [
    {
      "names": [
        "read", "write", "open", "close", "stat", "fstat", "lstat",
        "poll", "lseek", "mmap", "mprotect", "munmap", "brk",
        "rt_sigaction", "rt_sigprocmask", "rt_sigreturn",
        "ioctl", "pread64", "pwrite64", "readv", "writev",
        "access", "pipe", "select", "sched_yield", "mremap",
        "msync", "mincore", "madvise", "dup", "dup2", "nanosleep",
        "getpid", "sendfile", "socket", "connect", "accept",
        "sendto", "recvfrom", "sendmsg", "recvmsg", "shutdown",
        "bind", "listen", "getsockname", "getpeername",
        "socketpair", "setsockopt", "getsockopt", "clone",
        "fork", "vfork", "execve", "exit", "wait4", "kill",
        "uname", "fcntl", "flock", "fsync", "fdatasync",
        "truncate", "ftruncate", "getdents", "getcwd", "chdir",
        "rename", "mkdir", "rmdir", "creat", "link", "unlink",
        "symlink", "readlink", "chmod", "fchmod", "chown",
        "fchown", "lchown", "umask", "gettimeofday", "getrlimit",
        "getrusage", "sysinfo", "times", "getuid", "syslog",
        "getgid", "setuid", "setgid", "geteuid", "getegid",
        "setpgid", "getppid", "getpgrp", "setsid", "setreuid",
        "setregid", "getgroups", "setgroups", "setresuid",
        "getresuid", "setresgid", "getresgid", "getpgid",
        "setfsuid", "setfsgid", "getsid", "capget", "capset",
        "rt_sigpending", "rt_sigtimedwait", "rt_sigqueueinfo",
        "rt_sigsuspend", "sigaltstack", "utime", "mknod",
        "uselib", "personality", "ustat", "statfs", "fstatfs",
        "getpriority", "setpriority", "sched_setparam",
        "sched_getparam", "sched_setscheduler", "sched_getscheduler",
        "sched_get_priority_max", "sched_get_priority_min",
        "sched_rr_get_interval", "mlock", "munlock", "mlockall",
        "munlockall", "vhangup", "pivot_root", "prctl",
        "arch_prctl", "adjtimex", "setrlimit", "chroot",
        "sync", "acct", "settimeofday", "mount", "umount2",
        "getdents64", "set_tid_address", "restart_syscall",
        "semtimedop", "fadvise64", "timer_create", "timer_settime",
        "timer_gettime", "timer_getoverrun", "timer_delete",
        "clock_settime", "clock_gettime", "clock_getres",
        "clock_nanosleep", "exit_group", "epoll_wait",
        "epoll_ctl", "tgkill", "utimes", "mbind", "set_mempolicy",
        "get_mempolicy", "mq_open", "mq_unlink", "mq_timedsend",
        "mq_timedreceive", "mq_notify", "mq_getsetattr",
        "waitid", "ioprio_set", "ioprio_get", "inotify_init",
        "inotify_add_watch", "inotify_rm_watch", "migrate_pages",
        "openat", "mkdirat", "mknodat", "fchownat", "futimesat",
        "newfstatat", "unlinkat", "renameat", "linkat", "symlinkat",
        "readlinkat", "fchmodat", "faccessat", "pselect6",
        "ppoll", "unshare", "set_robust_list", "get_robust_list",
        "splice", "tee", "sync_file_range", "vmsplice",
        "move_pages", "utimensat", "epoll_pwait", "signalfd",
        "timerfd_create", "eventfd", "fallocate",
        "timerfd_settime", "timerfd_gettimerfd", "accept4",
        "signalfd4", "eventfd2", "epoll_create1", "dup3",
        "pipe2", "inotify_init1", "preadv", "pwritev",
        "rt_tgsigqueueinfo", "perf_event_open", "recvmmsg",
        "fanotify_init", "fanotify_mark", "prlimit64",
        "name_to_handle_at", "open_by_handle_at", "clock_adjtime",
        "syncfs", "sendmmsg", "setns", "getcpu",
        "process_vm_readv", "process_vm_writev", "kcmp",
        "finit_module", "sched_setattr", "sched_getattr",
        "renameat2", "seccomp", "getrandom", "memfd_create",
        "kexec_file_load", "bpf", "execveat", "userfaultfd",
        "membarrier", "mlock2", "copy_file_range", "preadv2",
        "pwritev2", "pkey_mprotect", "pkey_alloc", "pkey_free",
        "statx", "io_pgetevents", "rseq"
      ],
      "action": "SCMP_ACT_ALLOW"
    }
  ]
}
Monitoring Container Behaviour
Even with all flags set, monitoring what containers actually do reveals unexpected behaviour and helps tune the seccomp profile:

# Use strace inside the container to log syscalls (dev/debug only)
docker run --rm --cap-add SYS_PTRACE \
  code-sandbox:latest \
  strace -e trace=network,file python3 -c "import numpy"

# Use Falco (runtime security) to alert on suspicious container activity
# Example Falco rule (falco_rules.yaml):
# - rule: Outbound network from sandbox
#   desc: A sandbox container made an outbound connection
#   condition: >
#     evt.type = connect and container.name startswith "sandbox-"
#   output: "Outbound network from sandbox (container=%container.name ip=%fd.rip)"
#   priority: WARNING
Testing the Container Sandbox
Summary

Containers isolate code execution through four cooperative Linux kernel features: namespaces give the process its own view of the filesystem, network, and PID space; cgroups enforce memory, CPU, and process count limits; seccomp filters block dangerous syscalls; and capability drops remove kernel privileges. The combination — docker run with --network none, --read-only, --memory, --cap-drop ALL, --security-opt no-new-privileges — provides strong isolation at the cost of 1–3 seconds of startup latency. Pre-warming a container pool reduces this to milliseconds for interactive agents.
